TASK: archetypes

TASK RUBRIC:
Use Jungian ontology first. Accepted archetype labels must be one of: Self, Ego, Shadow, Persona, Anima, Animus, Great Mother, Wise Guide, Trickster, Child, Hero, OrphanExile.
Descriptive labels may appear only as archetype_gloss and must not replace the canonical Jungian archetype field.
Movement must be one of: fragmentation, inflation, descent, ordeal, projection, persona_fracture, shadow_confrontation, anima_animus_encounter, integration, emergence_of_self.
Resolve subject identity using the wider narrative corpus when possible. Do not leave generic labels like 'the man' as accepted subjects.
If the subject remains unresolved after context resolution, use only controlled placeholders.

EVIDENCE REQUIREMENTS:
- Every observation must cite source evidence using render_para_key plus an exact quote.
- Omit low-confidence claims instead of padding output.
- Empty observations are valid.
- Valid empty response is: {"observations":[]}
- Do not force a claim.
- Use the full narrative cognition capsule for consistency across the local corpus, but never treat context as source evidence.

    {
      "render_para_key": "rp_1b81ea2d1676e70d59ae45513f4dd924",

      "text_sha256": "ea1cab492e7f3e3b25349ad87a1cf4f75e76ef8e1283ac61f1dac0d03c714e8c",
      "text": "You cannot save someone who refuses saving. I know this for a fact, having watched grief work on people for longer than you might imagine. Some people throw everything away when they lose the thing they love most—burn it all, scatter the ashes, start fresh with empty hands. Others keep every thread, every cracked vessel, every scrap of parchment with nothing written on it, and pull it tighter and tighter until there is no room left to breathe. Aviel was one of those. Which is relevant because it is exactly what his son was creeping through the dark to undo."
    }
  ]
}

      "text_sha256": "ea1cab492e7f3e3b25349ad87a1cf4f75e76ef8e1283ac61f1dac0d03c714e8c",
      "text": "You cannot save someone who refuses saving. I know this for a fact, having watched grief work on people for longer than you might imagine. Some people throw everything away when they lose the thing they love most—burn it all, scatter the ashes, start fresh with empty hands. Others keep every thread, every cracked vessel, every scrap of parchment with nothing written on it, and pull it tighter and tighter until there is no room left to breathe. Aviel was one of those. Which is relevant because it is exactly what his son was creeping through the dark to undo."
    }
  ]
}

      "text_sha256": "a428d9f8b04e9b772cc80750bb76be0b5c27f11552ebb4cd3998884191c430e9",
      "text": "On a night when the moon was full on its third day, a boy with kind eyes moved through the monument his father's madness had made. His name was Dan. Sixteen, with messy black hair that tufted upward no matter how many times he patted it down or slicked it back. The torch trembled in his hand—the frail flame flickered not from wind but from the percussion against his ribs, casting suspicious shadows down the narrow hallway. Each barefoot step through the refuse was a wager against sound: one shard of pottery, one brittle bone breaking beneath his weight, and the house would know."
    }
  ]
}

      "text_sha256": "a428d9f8b04e9b772cc80750bb76be0b5c27f11552ebb4cd3998884191c430e9",
      "text": "On a night when the moon was full on its third day, a boy with kind eyes moved through the monument his father's madness had made. His name was Dan. Sixteen, with messy black hair that tufted upward no matter how many times he patted it down or slicked it back. The torch trembled in his hand—the frail flame flickered not from wind but from the percussion against his ribs, casting suspicious shadows down the narrow hallway. Each barefoot step through the refuse was a wager against sound: one shard of pottery, one brittle bone breaking beneath his weight, and the house would know."
    }
  ]
}

      "text_sha256": "ab33602236b6a562e233765f72456ae960dcf4c5aba31fdc12808a158aabf62f",
      "text": "The smell of the place was a terrible architecture—designed, it seemed, by someone who had thought very carefully about what it would take to make a person stop wanting to breathe. Sulfur and sweet rot and the patient mineral cold of turned earth. The still patience of serpents in a garden. Rotting textiles—Zuna's sacred threads left mid-shuttle—had unraveled into mildew and draped over discarded animal bones, making a dry rustling sound when disturbed that was the sound of all life giving up its last argument with death. The threads had once been extraordinary things. You could still see what they had been if you looked with the right kind of attention: crimson like pomegranate seeds split open, saffron like desert flowers at dawn, emerald like new wheat pushing through soil, amber like honey held to sunlight, violet like the sky at dusk, rose like oleander petals after rain. Every color under heaven woven together in patterns that had made people stop in the street and stare. Every color, that is, except the three she never touched—never blue, never purple, never scarlet—and she had never explained why, but Dan had understood in the wordless way children understand holy things that some colors were not for human hands."
    }
  ]
}

      "text_sha256": "ab33602236b6a562e233765f72456ae960dcf4c5aba31fdc12808a158aabf62f",
      "text": "The smell of the place was a terrible architecture—designed, it seemed, by someone who had thought very carefully about what it would take to make a person stop wanting to breathe. Sulfur and sweet rot and the patient mineral cold of turned earth. The still patience of serpents in a garden. Rotting textiles—Zuna's sacred threads left mid-shuttle—had unraveled into mildew and draped over discarded animal bones, making a dry rustling sound when disturbed that was the sound of all life giving up its last argument with death. The threads had once been extraordinary things. You could still see what they had been if you looked with the right kind of attention: crimson like pomegranate seeds split open, saffron like desert flowers at dawn, emerald like new wheat pushing through soil, amber like honey held to sunlight, violet like the sky at dusk, rose like oleander petals after rain. Every color under heaven woven together in patterns that had made people stop in the street and stare. Every color, that is, except the three she never touched—never blue, never purple, never scarlet—and she had never explained why, but Dan had understood in the wordless way children understand holy things that some colors were not for human hands."
    }
  ]
}

      "text_sha256": "ab33602236b6a562e233765f72456ae960dcf4c5aba31fdc12808a158aabf62f",
      "text": "The smell of the place was a terrible architecture—designed, it seemed, by someone who had thought very carefully about what it would take to make a person stop wanting to breathe. Sulfur and sweet rot and the patient mineral cold of turned earth. The still patience of serpents in a garden. Rotting textiles—Zuna's sacred threads left mid-shuttle—had unraveled into mildew and draped over discarded animal bones, making a dry rustling sound when disturbed that was the sound of all life giving up its last argument with death. The threads had once been extraordinary things. You could still see what they had been if you looked with the right kind of attention: crimson like pomegranate seeds split open, saffron like desert flowers at dawn, emerald like new wheat pushing through soil, amber like honey held to sunlight, violet like the sky at dusk, rose like oleander petals after rain. Every color under heaven woven together in patterns that had made people stop in the street and stare. Every color, that is, except the three she never touched—never blue, never purple, never scarlet—and she had never explained why, but Dan had understood in the wordless way children understand holy things that some colors were not for human hands."
    }
  ]

      "text_sha256": "ab33602236b6a562e233765f72456ae960dcf4c5aba31fdc12808a158aabf62f",
      "text": "The smell of the place was a terrible architecture—designed, it seemed, by someone who had thought very carefully about what it would take to make a person stop wanting to breathe. Sulfur and sweet rot and the patient mineral cold of turned earth. The still patience of serpents in a garden. Rotting textiles—Zuna's sacred threads left mid-shuttle—had unraveled into mildew and draped over discarded animal bones, making a dry rustling sound when disturbed that was the sound of all life giving up its last argument with death. The threads had once been extraordinary things. You could still see what they had been if you looked with the right kind of attention: crimson like pomegranate seeds split open, saffron like desert flowers at dawn, emerald like new wheat pushing through soil, amber like honey held to sunlight, violet like the sky at dusk, rose like oleander petals after rain. Every color under heaven woven together in patterns that had made people stop in the street and stare. Every color, that is, except the three she never touched—never blue, never purple, never scarlet—and she had never explained why, but Dan had understood in the wordless way children understand holy things that some colors were not for human hands."
    }
  ]

      "text_sha256": "ab33602236b6a562e233765f72456ae960dcf4c5aba31fdc12808a158aabf62f",
      "text": "The smell of the place was a terrible architecture—designed, it seemed, by someone who had thought very carefully about what it would take to make a person stop wanting to breathe. Sulfur and sweet rot and the patient mineral cold of turned earth. The still patience of serpents in a garden. Rotting textiles—Zuna's sacred threads left mid-shuttle—had unraveled into mildew and draped over discarded animal bones, making a dry rustling sound when disturbed that was the sound of all life giving up its last argument with death. The threads had once been extraordinary things. You could still see what they had been if you looked with the right kind of attention: crimson like pomegranate seeds split open, saffron like desert flowers at dawn, emerald like new wheat pushing through soil, amber like honey held to sunlight, violet like the sky at dusk, rose like oleander petals after rain. Every color under heaven woven together in patterns that had made people stop in the street and stare. Every color, that is, except the three she never touched—never blue, never purple, never scarlet—and she had never explained why, but Dan had understood in the wordless way children understand holy things that some colors were not for human hands."
    }
  ]

      "text_sha256": "f02f62214932cf7c22c803c701c83e88b1b6d2581c82d5bb74dd20a48af6fc4f",
      "text": "Dan had counted eighteen visible pottery shards one morning, and something about that number had made him stop counting there, as if eighteen marked the boundary between what still counted as life and what had crossed over entirely. The failed records lay everywhere—parchment and crumbling wax tablets, stripped clean by mold that had developed what could only be called ambition. Ruined astrolabes in the corners, their rings seized into arrangements pointing toward no coordinate in heaven or earth. Desiccated fruits clinging to their seeds. Water skins cracked and smelling of stale leather. Even the well had stopped remembering how to hold water."
    }
  ]
}

      "text_sha256": "f02f62214932cf7c22c803c701c83e88b1b6d2581c82d5bb74dd20a48af6fc4f",
      "text": "Dan had counted eighteen visible pottery shards one morning, and something about that number had made him stop counting there, as if eighteen marked the boundary between what still counted as life and what had crossed over entirely. The failed records lay everywhere—parchment and crumbling wax tablets, stripped clean by mold that had developed what could only be called ambition. Ruined astrolabes in the corners, their rings seized into arrangements pointing toward no coordinate in heaven or earth. Desiccated fruits clinging to their seeds. Water skins cracked and smelling of stale leather. Even the well had stopped remembering how to hold water."
    }
  ]
}

      "text_sha256": "96d81d5a674d0105edc8a15f5667bbb4696fbd3ae76a432679d4dd199e07181b",
      "text": "Through the wall slit, the stars of Hebron burned in their old patterns above the hills. His mother had taught him they were the closest thing to the unseen source—that original light from which everything borrowed its brief existence and to which everything returned. And his dreams had always found their way there. Not the shapeless kind. Deliberate. He had dreamed of climbing endless stairs in the dark, his legs never tiring, the air growing thinner and cleaner with every step. Of jumping and not coming back down. Of spreading his arms and rising until the whole dark earth curved below him like a broken plate. Everyone who has ever woken from that kind of dream and found a ceiling above them instead of open sky will understand what he was after."
    }
  ]
}

  "context_pack_excerpt": "a6ca39647c5b7267cd8c2d7234c956a69569fe0 SOURCE_PATH: /sdcard/Documents/WeightOfTheSky_Project/00_LORE_VAULT/New_Synopsis-_The_Weight_of_the_Sky.txt HASHER_PATH: docs/agent_context/source_drop/hasher_context_v1/04_new_synopsis_the_weight_of_the_sky.txt ================================================================================ The Weight of the Sky - Full Manuscript Synopsis (New Synopsis) The story begins in Hebron in 1003 BCE, a time when King David ruled over Judah, but the Philistines, with their iron weapons, still held significant power. In this tumultuous era, we meet a young boy named Dan, whose home is a physical manifestation of his father Aviel's grief-fueled hoarding after the death of Dan's mother, Zuna. Unlike his father, Dan is a dreamwalker, a rare visionary who can consciously control his dreams. He sees the dreamscape not just as a subconscious world, but as a universal realm made of \"dust,\" the smallest unit of creativity. The dreamscape is layered, from the lowest depths of Pride and Gluttony to the highest reaches of Truth and a mysterious Light. In the dreamscape, all things are possible because all things exist within it. The novel establishes a crucial metaphysical conflict: some people believe the \"low gods,\" such as Dagon, Baal, and El, hold up the Earth from beneath, making them the most powerful. Others believe the \"high gods\" who watch from above—Yahweh, Courage, and Hope—are the true divine authorities. Dan's journey is a physical manifestation of his spiritual quest to ascend and find the highest God. One night, Dan enters the dreamscape to send a message to his sleeping father. He forms creative \"dust\" into rocks that fall around Aviel. As his father instinctively hoards them, Dan's message becomes clear: his father is being consumed by his earthly possessions. Only when the rocks threaten to suffocate him does Aviel reluctantly let go, and Dan ends the dream. Aviel, having brought the gods of Philistia with him, worships Dagon, the fish god of abundance. Angered by Dan’s interference, Dagon appears to Dan in an uncontrolled dream. Dan finds himself on a ship in an endless ocean, face-to-face with the deity. Dagon attempts to coerce him, but Dan remains steadfast, recalling Dagon’s past specific defeats: how his statue crumbled before the Ark of the Covenant and how his temple was destroyed by Samson. Dagon dismisses this, claiming the \"realm of the living cannot touch the realm of the Dead.\" Dan calls Dagon a \"deity of death,\" to which Dagon replies, \"Look at the war within you and the wars around you; death is ever upon you.       "text_sha256": "96d81d5a674d0105edc8a15f5667bbb4696fbd3ae76a432679d4dd199e07181b",
      "text": "Through the wall slit, the stars of Hebron burned in their old patterns above the hills. His mother had taught him they were the closest thing to the unseen source—that original light from which everything borrowed its brief existence and to which everything returned. And his dreams had always found their way there. Not the shapeless kind. Deliberate. He had dreamed of climbing endless stairs in the dark, his legs never tiring, the air growing thinner and cleaner with every step. Of jumping and not coming back down. Of spreading his arms and rising until the whole dark earth curved below him like a broken plate. Everyone who has ever woken from that kind of dream and found a ceiling above them instead of open sky will understand what he was after."
    }
  ]
}

  "context_pack_excerpt": "a6ca39647c5b7267cd8c2d7234c956a69569fe0 SOURCE_PATH: /sdcard/Documents/WeightOfTheSky_Project/00_LORE_VAULT/New_Synopsis-_The_Weight_of_the_Sky.txt HASHER_PATH: docs/agent_context/source_drop/hasher_context_v1/04_new_synopsis_the_weight_of_the_sky.txt ================================================================================ The Weight of the Sky - Full Manuscript Synopsis (New Synopsis) The story begins in Hebron in 1003 BCE, a time when King David ruled over Judah, but the Philistines, with their iron weapons, still held significant power. In this tumultuous era, we meet a young boy named Dan, whose home is a physical manifestation of his father Aviel's grief-fueled hoarding after the death of Dan's mother, Zuna. Unlike his father, Dan is a dreamwalker, a rare visionary who can consciously control his dreams. He sees the dreamscape not just as a subconscious world, but as a universal realm made of \"dust,\" the smallest unit of creativity. The dreamscape is layered, from the lowest depths of Pride and Gluttony to the highest reaches of Truth and a mysterious Light. In the dreamscape, all things are possible because all things exist within it. The novel establishes a crucial metaphysical conflict: some people believe the \"low gods,\" such as Dagon, Baal, and El, hold up the Earth from beneath, making them the most powerful. Others believe the \"high gods\" who watch from above—Yahweh, Courage, and Hope—are the true divine authorities. Dan's journey is a physical manifestation of his spiritual quest to ascend and find the highest God. One night, Dan enters the dreamscape to send a message to his sleeping father. He forms creative \"dust\" into rocks that fall around Aviel. As his father instinctively hoards them, Dan's message becomes clear: his father is being consumed by his earthly possessions. Only when the rocks threaten to suffocate him does Aviel reluctantly let go, and Dan ends the dream. Aviel, having brought the gods of Philistia with him, worships Dagon, the fish god of abundance. Angered by Dan’s interference, Dagon appears to Dan in an uncontrolled dream. Dan finds himself on a ship in an endless ocean, face-to-face with the deity. Dagon attempts to coerce him, but Dan remains steadfast, recalling Dagon’s past specific defeats: how his statue crumbled before the Ark of the Covenant and how his temple was destroyed by Samson. Dagon dismisses this, claiming the \"realm of the living cannot touch the realm of the Dead.\" Dan calls Dagon a \"deity of death,\" to which Dagon replies, \"Look at the war within you and the wars around you; death is ever upon you.       "text_sha256": "96d81d5a674d0105edc8a15f5667bbb4696fbd3ae76a432679d4dd199e07181b",
      "text": "Through the wall slit, the stars of Hebron burned in their old patterns above the hills. His mother had taught him they were the closest thing to the unseen source—that original light from which everything borrowed its brief existence and to which everything returned. And his dreams had always found their way there. Not the shapeless kind. Deliberate. He had dreamed of climbing endless stairs in the dark, his legs never tiring, the air growing thinner and cleaner with every step. Of jumping and not coming back down. Of spreading his arms and rising until the whole dark earth curved below him like a broken plate. Everyone who has ever woken from that kind of dream and found a ceiling above them instead of open sky will understand what he was after."
    }
  ]
}

  "context_pack_excerpt": "a6ca39647c5b7267cd8c2d7234c956a69569fe0 SOURCE_PATH: /sdcard/Documents/WeightOfTheSky_Project/00_LORE_VAULT/New_Synopsis-_The_Weight_of_the_Sky.txt HASHER_PATH: docs/agent_context/source_drop/hasher_context_v1/04_new_synopsis_the_weight_of_the_sky.txt ================================================================================ The Weight of the Sky - Full Manuscript Synopsis (New Synopsis) The story begins in Hebron in 1003 BCE, a time when King David ruled over Judah, but the Philistines, with their iron weapons, still held significant power. In this tumultuous era, we meet a young boy named Dan, whose home is a physical manifestation of his father Aviel's grief-fueled hoarding after the death of Dan's mother, Zuna. Unlike his father, Dan is a dreamwalker, a rare visionary who can consciously control his dreams. He sees the dreamscape not just as a subconscious world, but as a universal realm made of \"dust,\" the smallest unit of creativity. The dreamscape is layered, from the lowest depths of Pride and Gluttony to the highest reaches of Truth and a mysterious Light. In the dreamscape, all things are possible because all things exist within it. The novel establishes a crucial metaphysical conflict: some people believe the \"low gods,\" such as Dagon, Baal, and El, hold up the Earth from beneath, making them the most powerful. Others believe the \"high gods\" who watch from above—Yahweh, Courage, and Hope—are the true divine authorities. Dan's journey is a physical manifestation of his spiritual quest to ascend and find the highest God. One night, Dan enters the dreamscape to send a message to his sleeping father. He forms creative \"dust\" into rocks that fall around Aviel. As his father instinctively hoards them, Dan's message becomes clear: his father is being consumed by his earthly possessions. Only when the rocks threaten to suffocate him does Aviel reluctantly let go, and Dan ends the dream. Aviel, having brought the gods of Philistia with him, worships Dagon, the fish god of abundance. Angered by Dan’s interference, Dagon appears to Dan in an uncontrolled dream. Dan finds himself on a ship in an endless ocean, face-to-face with the deity. Dagon attempts to coerce him, but Dan remains steadfast, recalling Dagon’s past specific defeats: how his statue crumbled before the Ark of the Covenant and how his temple was destroyed by Samson. Dagon dismisses this, claiming the \"realm of the living cannot touch the realm of the Dead.\" Dan calls Dagon a \"deity of death,\" to which Dagon replies, \"Look at the war within you and the wars around you; death is ever upon you.       "text": "His mother, Zuna, had been a dreamwalker—rarer than it sounds and considerably more dangerous than it looks. She had taught Dan the same capacity before she went, which is to say: she had taught him to enter another person's sleep and move within it with deliberate intention. Some say such an ability belongs to those who consort with certain patient, curious watchers of the world—creatures of thread and careful observation whose tales are considerably longer than a ball of yarn. I will leave it at that."
    }

      "text": "His mother, Zuna, had been a dreamwalker—rarer than it sounds and considerably more dangerous than it looks. She had taught Dan the same capacity before she went, which is to say: she had taught him to enter another person's sleep and move within it with deliberate intention. Some say such an ability belongs to those who consort with certain patient, curious watchers of the world—creatures of thread and careful observation whose tales are considerably longer than a ball of yarn. I will leave it at that."
    }
